summary: A digital property management platform that allows Dubai real estate investors to receive rent in real time and manage their properties remotely. The app includes practical tools designed for foreign investors, such as contract reminders, contract review support, rent price adjustment tracking, property performance visibility, and the ability to request home services from one interface. However, the core feature is real-time rental payouts, which solve a major inefficiency in the Dubai real estate market. Today, tenants are often required to provide 1 pre-paid cheque (for the entire annual rent) to secure better rental prices. This is inconvenient for tenants because it requires committing large amounts of cash upfront. Landlords demand this structure because they are concerned about liquidity and want to secure their rental income as early as possible. With our application, landlords are settled in real time (up to four times per hour) directly to their wallets, while rent is collected from tenants on a quarterly basis through standard post-paid cheque structures. This removes the liquidity gap for landlords and eliminates the need for tenants to prepay large annual amounts, improving cash flow efficiency for both sides without changing existing market behavior. One Estate will be earning revenue by charging a commission from both tenants and investors. From tenants - for a more convenient payments (in the end of 3 month rent, rather than 1 annual pre-paid). From investors - as an app “subscription fee”. Total commission per rental property is estimated at 6% (4% tenants + 2% investors). This will be used for social media/website descriptions 4

summary: xNS is the naming and routing layer for Canton. Instead of copying opaque Canton party IDs, users and institutions use names like alice.canton or desk1.fx.bankx.canton. Wallets and apps call xNS to turn those names into the correct Canton party and routing metadata before a transaction is sent. Relationship to CNS (and why xNS is not a CNS wrapper) CNS is the canonical, low-level party registry on Canton: “which legal/technical entity does this party represent?” xNS is an application-level naming and routing fabric: “what should this name mean today across wallets, desks, products and policies?” xNS uses CNS as an input but it is not a 1:1 alias or UI skin over CNS MVP (Loop + ENS connectivity via MetaMask) In the MVP, we have integrated with our partners at 5N Loop. A Loop user can: Claim a .canton name In Loop, a user searches for an available name (e.g. alice.canton), claims it, and sets a default destination (their Canton wallet). Policy checks and a reserved list apply. Send to names, not parties When anyone types alice.canton in Loop, the wallet calls xNS, which returns the current Canton party and routing metadata (derived from CNS where relevant). The user only sees and approves alice.canton. Link a .canton name to ENS via MetaMask The user connects an Ethereum wallet (e.g. via MetaMask), proves control of an ENS name like alice.eth, and links it to alice.canton. Respect reserved and sensitive names xNS enforces a reserved list and simple policies so obvious trademarks, existing institutional names and abusive terms are not available through the standard retail flow. These are handled via controlled enterprise or manual processes. Under the hood, only lifecycle events (create, update, transfer, verification, policy changes) are on-ledger. High-volume name resolution is off-ledger. Beyond the MVP, xNS adds structured enterprise namespaces, optional verification and richer metadata, but always as a separate application layer built on top of CNS, not a CNS re-skin. Beyond the MVP, xNS extends in three main directions: Enterprise namespaces: Structured hierarchies like bankx.canton, fx.trading.bankx.canton with policies for reserving, delegating and blocking names. Verification and richer metadata: Optional verification badges from recognised verifiers, plus basic metadata (e.g. supported products/roles) to make names safer and more informative. Governance and decentralisation: Gradual migration of key parameters (reserved lists, verifier sets, some marker logic) into governed on-ledger contracts, evolving toward a neutral, multi-stakeholder naming and routing standard for Canton.
